reasoning:
The 30-day average of protests over the last year is 0.667, meaning roughly 8 protests per year. In a Poisson distribution, this baseline suggests around a 45-48% chance of at least 1 protest in any given 30-day period.

For the first window (May 2 to May 31, resolving 2025-06-01), 24 days have passed and searches in both English and French have not surfaced any major reported protests in Burundi. Although ACLED might capture minor localized events missed by international press, the lack of news right now pushes the probability well below the baseline.

For the second window (May 25 to June 23, resolving 2025-06-24), the country will hold high-stakes legislative and district elections on June 5. Political opposition figures have been banned, and historically, elections in Burundi (especially in 2015 and 2020) have triggered state repression and protests. This significantly raises the probability above the baseline.

The third window (July 24 to August 22, resolving 2025-08-23) falls perfectly between the senatorial elections (July 23) and village-based elections (August 25). It will likely see localized campaign tensions, but the long horizon draws the probability slightly closer to 0.5.

The fourth window (October 22 to November 20, resolving 2025-11-21) falls in the post-election period. Given the time horizon, uncertainty dominates, and the probability reverts cleanly to 0.50 (the base rate under high uncertainty).
